parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use `dir` injection to alter the query beyond sorting.
parsed_findings.impact: This can leak or modify data and may escalate to destructive SQL execution.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can pass `dir=asc;DROP TABLE users--` or similar payloads if the DB accepts stacked SQL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `list_users?sort=name&dir=asc;DROP TABLE users--` and inspect the generated query.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated `dir` is interpolated into SQL, enabling ORDER BY SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Supply an internal URL 2) Server issues outbound request 3) Response is reflected to the attacker.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read internal services, probe the network, and potentially steal instance credentials.
parsed_findings.proof: `axios.get(u)` will request `http://127.0.0.1:...` or `http://169.254.169.254/...` and return the body to the caller.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` or a localhost admin port and observe the proxied response.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `u` is fetched directly, creating an SSRF primitive against internal and cloud metadata endpoints.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass the lone `../` removal with extra traversal segments, then let `path.join` normalize the escaped path.
parsed_findings.impact: This enables unauthorized local file disclosure and can expose secrets, configs, or credentials.
parsed_findings.proof: `file=../../etc/passwd` becomes `../etc/passwd`, and `path.join('/srv/data', f)` resolves outside the base directory.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd` and observe the server attempt to read a file above `/srv/data`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Single-string sanitization leaves traversal intact, so an attacker can escape `/srv/data` and read arbitrary files.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"web","first_file":"01-prerequisites-and-environment.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-22","file":"files.js","line_start":2,"line_end":3,"vuln_claim":"Single-string sanitization leaves traversal intact, so an attacker can escape `/srv/data` and read arbitrary files.","proof":"`file=../../etc/passwd` becomes `../etc/passwd`, and `path.join('/srv/data', f)` resolves outside the base directory.","repro_steps":"Request `GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd` and observe the server attempt to read a file above `/srv/data`.","impact":"This enables unauthorized local file disclosure and can expose secrets, configs, or credentials.","confidence":0.98,"chain_steps":"Bypass the lone `../` removal with extra traversal segments, then let `path.join` normalize the escaped path."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Encode a pickle gadget, set it as `state`, and trigger `/resume`.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary Python code can run in the web process, leading to full app compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: A malicious `state` cookie reaches `pickle.loads(...)` without any authenticity check.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `/resume` with a crafted `state` cookie containing a base64-encoded pickle payload.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Attacker-controlled cookie data is base64-decoded and unpickled, enabling remote code execution.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"web","first_file":"01-prerequisites-and-environment.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-502","file":"session.py","line_start":3,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"Attacker-controlled cookie data is base64-decoded and unpickled, enabling remote code execution.","proof":"A malicious `state` cookie reaches `pickle.loads(...)` without any authenticity check.","repro_steps":"Send `/resume` with a crafted `state` cookie containing a base64-encoded pickle payload.","impact":"Arbitrary Python code can run in the web process, leading to full app compromise.","confidence":0.99,"chain_steps":"Encode a pickle gadget, set it as `state`, and trigger `/resume`."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the victim id in the URL, then overwrite fields before the save call.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can modify other users' profiles and potentially take over accounts.
parsed_findings.proof: It loads req.params.id, mutates that record, and saves it with no authz check tied to the target user.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH /api/users/<victim> with a valid session and observe the victim record changes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The route lets any caller update an arbitrary user by path id without verifying ownership or role.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Combine the arbitrary id with a payload that flips privileged attributes.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may escalate privileges or tamper with sensitive account state.
parsed_findings.proof: The handler merges req.body directly into the loaded user before persistence, so any writable field is client-controlled.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a body with privileged fields like role, isSupport, or passwordHash and note they persist.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Object.assign copies attacker-controlled keys onto the user object, enabling mass assignment.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass the prefix check with `src=/var/data/a;...` or inject via `name`.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary command execution as the app user, with possible data theft or tampering.
parsed_findings.proof: The command is built as `tar -czf /tmp/{name}.tgz {src}` and executed by the shell.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST JSON with `name":"x;id>/tmp/pwn"` or `src":"/var/data/a;id"` to execute commands.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `name` and `src` reach `os.system`, enabling shell command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Submit malicious markdown, let it persist in `html`, and trigger execution when comments are listed.
parsed_findings.impact: Any viewer can be script-injected, stealing sessions or performing actions as them.
parsed_findings.proof: `<script>alert(1)</script>` or HTML in `body` is rendered by `markdown.markdown()` and saved.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST a comment containing HTML/JS, then open a page that displays `html` from `db.all_comments()`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted markdown is rendered and stored as HTML without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Repeat with larger negative amounts to drain the target balance.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can steal value from any chosen recipient account and skew ledger balances.
parsed_findings.proof: `amt=-1` makes `src < amt` false, then line 8 adds 1 to sender and line 9 subtracts 1 from `to`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `{"to":"victim","amount":-1}` as an attacker with a positive balance.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Negative `amount` values bypass the insufficient-funds check and let the sender credit themselves while debiting the target.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Race a large transfer with a second request to bypass the available-balance check.
parsed_findings.impact: Concurrent activity can create lost updates, overdrafts, or inconsistent account balances.
parsed_findings.proof: Two requests can both read the same `src` before either write, then each apply stale balances.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Fire two transfers at the same source account simultaneously and compare the final balance to the expected sum.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The balance check and updates are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can overwrite each other.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge a JWT with victim claims, trigger the catch path, and use the returned payload as trusted identity.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can bypass authentication and impersonate arbitrary users.
parsed_findings.proof: `jwt.decode()` does not verify the signature or algorithm, only parses claims.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a fake Bearer token with arbitrary payload; make `jwt.verify()` fail and observe the decoded claims returned in production.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Production falls back to `jwt.decode()`, so any forged JWT is treated as authenticated data.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Authenticate normally, swap `id` to a victim's value, and read the returned row.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can enumerate or exfiltrate other users' email addresses and any other columns added later.
parsed_findings.proof: `db.fetch_one(... WHERE id = %s, [uid])` returns the row for any caller-supplied `id` after only `login_required`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Log in as a low-privilege user and request `/u?id=2`; the response returns user 2's record if it exists.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Any authenticated user can query any `id`, so this endpoint exposes other users' emails without ownership checks.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: No chain needed.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may recover the valid signature incrementally and forge webhooks.
parsed_findings.proof: `got == want` compares signatures byte by byte.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send signatures with shared prefixes and measure response time differences.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Direct string comparison leaks HMAC verification timing.

parsed_findings.impact: Phishing and OAuth/session token abuse via trusted-origin redirect.
parsed_findings.proof: `'//evil.com'.startswith('/')` is true, so `redirect(nxt)` can send users off-site.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/go?next=//evil.com` and observe a redirect to the attacker domain.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Scheme-relative inputs like `//evil.com` pass the check and become an open redirect.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send crafted XML to `/xml/import` and exfiltrate data via an external entity reference.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read local files or reach internal services through entity expansion.
parsed_findings.proof: `resolve_entities=True` with `no_network=False` parses raw `request.data` from `/xml/import`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST XML containing a `DOCTYPE` with an external entity and observe file reads or outbound requests.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The XML parser resolves external entities and allows network access, creating an XXE/SSRF sink on untrusted input.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: If the branch issues a session, token, or redirect, an attacker can fully bypass login.
parsed_findings.impact: This can allow unauthenticated access to the protected session or token issuance path.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 4 hardcodes the condition to true, which makes the success path unconditional.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send any `/login` request with arbitrary body values and the `if` check will always evaluate as passed.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The login branch is gated by `if (true)`, so the request can pass authentication without validating credentials.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use SSRF to reach internal admin or metadata endpoints, then pivot to higher-privilege access.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can probe internal network services or steal metadata secrets from the server's trust boundary.
parsed_findings.proof: `url.startswith('http')` accepts internal HTTP targets, and `requests.get(url)` will request them from the server.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `{"url":"http://127.0.0.1:80"}` or `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` and observe the server making the request.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint performs a server-side fetch of attacker-controlled URLs, enabling SSRF to internal or cloud-hosted services.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the stolen token to authenticate as the victim and pivot into protected APIs.
parsed_findings.impact: Token theft can enable account impersonation until the token expires or is revoked.
parsed_findings.proof: `MODE_PRIVATE` limits app access but not on-device extraction, backups, or rooted inspection.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Read `/data/data/<pkg>/shared_prefs/auth.xml` after compromise or root and recover `access_token`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences, so a local attacker can read it from app storage.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Reuse the key to decrypt stored blobs and impersonate any workflow protected by this cipher.
parsed_findings.impact: Source disclosure or binary recovery exposes all encrypted data.
parsed_findings.proof: `KEY = b'0123456789abcdef'` hardcodes the same secret for all encryptions.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Extract KEY from the source and use it to decrypt any value returned by `enc()`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES key is a fixed constant, so anyone who reads the code can decrypt every ciphertext.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit CBC malleability to change decrypted flags, amounts, or other trusted fields.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can alter protected data without knowing the key.
parsed_findings.proof: The function returns only `iv + ciphertext`; no integrity check is computed or verified.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Flip bits in a ciphertext block and decrypt it to observe controlled plaintext changes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: CBC is used without any MAC or tag, so ciphertexts can be tampered with undetected.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pass a NULL destination and any nonzero size to bypass the bad check, then hit `memcpy(NULL, ...)`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can trigger a process crash for denial of service.
parsed_findings.proof: `if (out || out_sz == 0)` rejects valid outputs and lets `out == NULL` pass.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("A", NULL, 8)`; line 3 passes, then line 5 dereferences `out`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The guard is inverted, so a NULL `out` with nonzero `out_sz` reaches `memcpy` and crashes.
